figures.caption: TABLE I QUANTITATIVE EVALUATION OF LIORNET UNDER DIFFERENT CONFIGURATIONS. POSTPROCESS WAS APPLIED WITH THE THEORETICAL SNOW SENSING LIMIT OF 71.23M. (UW: UNCERTAINTY-BASED WEIGHTING, PP: POSTPROCESSING, DS: DEEP SUPERVISION)
TABLE II QUANTITATIVE EVALUATION ON THE WADS DATASET. POSTPROCESS WAS APPLIED USING THE OPTIMAL RANGE OF 20M.
Fig. 1. Categorization by approach [8]. LIORNet shown at the center is the first attempt to unify all three strategies.
Fig. 4. False-positive snow data before and after applying the rule-based postprocessing (considering LiDAR height and the theoretical snow-sensing distance).
Fig. 2. Overall LIORNet framework: (a) data processing pipeline, (b) network architecture, and backbone configurations using (c) U-Net and (d) U-Net++.
Fig. 6. Performance evaluation on real-world data (a) Raw, (b) DROR, (c) LIOR, (d) D-LIOR, (e) LiSnowNet, (f) LIORNet.
Fig. 5. Comparison of performance on the (A) WADS and (B) CADC datasets. (a) Raw, (b) DROR, (c) LIOR, (d) D-LIOR, (e) LiSnowNet, (f) LIORNet.

Fig. 3. Visualization of conditional pseudo-label point updates. Candidate snow points are sequentially refined through intensity (Cond1), reflectivity (Cond2), edge (Cond3), and sparsity (Cond4) conditions resulting in the final pseudo-labeled snow points.
